# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain how a transfer function is represented in MATLAB using the `tf` command. Provide the syntax for a first-order system with $K_p = 2$ and $\tau_p = 5$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In MATLAB, a transfer function is represented using the `tf` command from the Control System Toolbox. The general syntax is:
```
G = tf(numerator, denominator)
```
where `numerator` and `denominator` are coefficient vectors of the polynomials in descending powers of $s$.

For a **first-order system** with $K_p = 2$ and $\tau_p = 5$:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{2}{5s + 1}$$
The MATLAB code is:
```matlab
num = 2;
den = [5, 1];
Gp = tf(num, den);
```
The variable `Gp` can then be used for analysis such as `step(Gp)`, `bode(Gp)`, and `pzmap(Gp)`. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three built-in MATLAB functions used to analyze the dynamic response of a closed-loop system. State the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `step(G)` | Plots the **unit step response** of a transfer function `G`. |
| 2 | `stepinfo(G)` | Returns a structure containing **performance indices** such as rise time, settling time, and overshoot. |
| 3 | `feedback(Gc, Gp)` | Computes the **closed-loop transfer function** for a unity-feedback system. |

Other useful functions include `bode(G)` for frequency response, `pzmap(G)` for pole-zero visualization, and `margin(G)` for stability margins. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — PI Control of a First-Order Process

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

A temperature control process is modeled as a **first-order system** with transfer function:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{2}{4s + 1}$$

A **PI controller** with $K_c = 1.5$ and $\tau_I = 6$ is implemented.

#### (a) Construct the closed-loop transfer function and write the MATLAB code to simulate its step response. Compute the percentage overshoot and settling time. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PI Controller transfer function:**
$$
G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = \frac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s} = \frac{1.5(6s + 1)}{6s}
$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{CL}(s) &= \frac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1.5(6s + 1)}{6s} \cdot \dfrac{2}{4s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{1.5(6s + 1)}{6s} \cdot \dfrac{2}{4s + 1}} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{1.5(6s + 1)(2)}{6s(4s + 1) + 1.5(6s + 1)(2)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3(6s + 1)}{24s^2 + 6s + 18s + 3} = \frac{3(6s + 1)}{24s^2 + 24s + 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplifying (dividing by 3):
$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{6s + 1}{8s^2 + 8s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — MATLAB Code:**
```matlab
% Process
Gp = tf(2, [4, 1]);
% PI Controller
Kc = 1.5; tau_I = 6;
Gc = Kc * tf([tau_I, 1], [tau_I, 0]);
% Closed-Loop
Gcl = feedback(Gc * Gp, 1);
% Step Response
t = 0:0.05:60;
step(Gcl, t); grid on;
title('PI-Controlled First-Order System');
% Performance Metrics
S = stepinfo(Gcl);
fprintf('Overshoot     = %.2f %%\n', S.Overshoot);
fprintf('Settling time = %.2f min\n', S.SettlingTime);
```

**Step 4 — Performance Metrics (Analytical Verification):**

Comparing $8s^2 + 8s + 1 = 0$ with $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:
$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow \omega_n = 0.354 \text{ rad/min}
$$
$$
2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \Rightarrow \zeta = \frac{1}{2\omega_n} = \frac{1}{0.707} \approx 0.707
$$

- **Overshoot:**
$$\%OS = 100 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-0.707 \pi}{\sqrt{1 - 0.5}}\right) = 100 \cdot e^{-3.14} \approx 4.3\%$$

- **Settling time (2%):**
$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{0.707 \times 0.354} \approx 16 \text{ min}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 2 for TF derivation, 3 for MATLAB code, 2 for performance computation)**

---

#### (b) Briefly describe how the same system can be implemented in Simulink. Mention the key blocks used. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

In **Simulink**, the closed-loop PI-controlled first-order system is implemented using the following blocks (typically found in the *Simulink Library Browser*):

| Block | Library Location | Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| **Step** | Sources | Step time = 0, Final value = 1 (unit step) |
| **Sum** | Math Operations | List of signs = `+|-` (computes error) |
| **PID Controller** | Continuous | Form = *Parallel*, P = 1.5, I = $K_c/\tau_I$ = 0.25, D = 0 |
| **Transfer Fcn** | Continuous | Numerator = `[2]`, Denominator = `[4 1]` |
| **Scope** | Sinks | To view output $y(t)$ |
| **To Workspace** | Sinks | To save data for further analysis |

**Implementation Steps:**

1. Open Simulink and create a new blank model.
2. Drag the **Step** block, set step time to 0 and amplitude to 1.
3. Place the **Sum** block to compute the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
4. Add the **PID Controller** block; set P = 1.5, I = 0.25, D = 0.
5. Insert the **Transfer Fcn** block representing $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{4s+1}$.
6. Connect the blocks in series: Step → Sum → PID → Transfer Fcn → Scope.
7. Branch the output line back to the negative input of the **Sum** block (feedback path).
8. Set simulation stop time to **60 seconds** and run the simulation.
9. Double-click the **Scope** to view the step response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of Simulink over MATLAB script:** Simulink allows you to add **nonlinearities** (e.g., valve saturation, dead zone, transport delay) and **disturbances** graphically without altering the underlying equations. **(7 marks distributed: 3 for block identification, 4 for implementation procedure)**

---

### Question B — PID Control of a Second-Order Process (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

A second-order underdamped process has the transfer function:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{4}{s^2 + 2s + 4}$$

A **PID controller** is to be designed with $K_c = 1.2$, $\tau_I = 2$, $\tau_D = 0.5$.

#### (a) Form the closed-loop transfer function and simulate the response in MATLAB. Comment on the stability using the pole locations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PID controller in transfer function form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_c(s) &= K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right) \\[4pt]
&= 1.2\left(1 + \frac{1}{2s} + 0.5s\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.2(2s)(0.5s + 1) + 1.2(0.5s + 1)\cdot 0.5s\,(\text{...})}{...}
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplicity, MATLAB's `pid` object handles the conversion:
```matlab
% Process
wn = 2; zeta = 0.5; Kp = 1;
Gp = tf(Kp * wn^2, [1, 2*zeta*wn, wn^2]);

% Corrected for given problem
Gp = tf(4, [1, 2, 4]);

% PID Controller
C = pid(1.2, 0.6, 0.6);   % Kc=1.2, Ki=Kc/tauI=0.6, Kd=Kc*tauD=0.6

% Closed-loop
Gcl = feedback(C * Gp, 1);

% Step response
step(Gcl); grid on;
S = stepinfo(Gcl);
fprintf('Overshoot = %.2f %%\n', S.Overshoot);
fprintf('Settling time = %.2f s\n', S.SettlingTime);

% Pole-zero map
figure; pzmap(Gcl); grid on;
title('Pole-Zero Map');
```

**Step 2 — Stability check via poles:**
- Compute the characteristic polynomial: $1 + G_c G_p = 0$.
- Use MATLAB `pole(Gcl)` to find pole locations.
- **Stable** if all poles lie in the **left half of the s-plane** ($\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$).

**Comments:**
- The derivative action $\tau_D s$ introduces a **zero** in the closed-loop transfer function, which speeds up the response.
- For this system, expect reduced overshoot compared to PI-only control and faster settling time.
- A small overshoot (typically 5–15%) with no steady-state error is expected.

**(7 marks: 2 for TF derivation, 3 for code, 2 for stability comment)**

---

#### (b) Compare the step responses of the system with P, PI, and PID controllers. Which controller gives the best performance and why? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A comparative MATLAB simulation produces three curves on the same plot:

```matlab
% Comparative analysis
Gp = tf(4, [1, 2, 4]);

% P Controller
P_ctrl = pid(1.2, 0, 0);
Gcl_P  = feedback(P_ctrl * Gp, 1);

% PI Controller
PI_ctrl = pid(1.2, 0.6, 0);
Gcl_PI  = feedback(PI_ctrl * Gp, 1);

% PID Controller
PID_ctrl = pid(1.2, 0.6, 0.6);
Gcl_PID  = feedback(PID_ctrl * Gp, 1);

% Plot all three
figure;
step(Gcl_P, Gcl_PI, Gcl_PID, 20);
legend('P-Control', 'PI-Control', 'PID-Control');
title('Comparison: P vs PI vs PID Control');
grid on;

% Metrics
fprintf('--- P-Control ---\n');    S1 = stepinfo(Gcl_P);
fprintf('Overshoot=%.2f %%, ts=%.2f s\n', S1.Overshoot, S1.SettlingTime);
fprintf('--- PI-Control ---\n');   S2 = stepinfo(Gcl_PI);
fprintf('Overshoot=%.2f %%, ts=%.2f s\n', S2.Overshoot, S2.SettlingTime);
fprintf('--- PID-Control ---\n');  S3 = stepinfo(Gcl_PID);
fprintf('Overshoot=%.2f %%, ts=%.2f s\n', S3.Overshoot, S3.SettlingTime);
```

**Qualitative Comparison Table (expected trends):**

| Metric | P-Control | PI-Control | PID-Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error | Large | Zero | Zero |
| Overshoot | Moderate | Higher | Reduced (due to D) |
| Settling time | Long | Moderate | **Shortest** |
| Stability margin | Best | Reduced | Restored by D |

**Conclusion:**
The **PID controller** provides the best overall performance:
- **Proportional** action gives fast initial response.
- **Integral** action eliminates steady-state error.
- **Derivative** action damps oscillations and anticipates future error.

However, **derivative action is sensitive to measurement noise**, so a low-pass filter is often added in practice. **(7 marks: 3 for comparative plot, 2 for tabulated metrics, 2 for conclusion with engineering justification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - ❌ Forgetting to use `tf(..., [..., 0])` for the integral term — this causes a **"singular matrix"** error in MATLAB.
> - ❌ Mixing up the parallel PID form $K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ with the standard form $K_c(1 + 1/\tau_I s + \tau_D s)$ — remember the conversions: $K_i = K_c/\tau_I$ and $K_d = K_c \tau_D$.
> - ❌ Applying the overshoot formula $e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ to **overdamped** ($\zeta \geq 1$) systems — the formula is valid **only for $0 < \zeta < 1$**.
> - ❌ In Simulink, leaving the **PID block's filter coefficient N** at default (100) when the system is slow — this introduces hidden dynamics.
> - ❌ Computing settling time using $4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ for a first-order system — this is a second-order approximation only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Implementation Topic**

- ✅ **MATLAB `tf` syntax:** `G = tf(num, den)` where vectors are in **descending powers of $s$**.
- ✅ **Closed-loop command:** `Gcl = feedback(Gc*Gp, 1)` for **unity negative feedback**.
- ✅ **Step response:** `step(G, t)` plots $y(t)$ for unit step input; `stepinfo(G)` returns rise time, settling time, overshoot.
- ✅ **PID parallel form:** `pid(Kp, Ki, Kd)` — internal conversions: $K_i = K_c/\tau_I$, $K_d = K_c \tau_D$.
- ✅ **Overshoot formula valid only for underdamped** second-order systems ($\zeta < 1$).
- ✅ **Settling time (2%):** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ — derived from $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_s} \approx 0.02$.
- ✅ **Simulink essentials:** `Step`, `Sum` (+|-), `PID Controller`, `Transfer Fcn`, `Scope` are the five key blocks.
- ✅ **Stability check:** All closed-loop poles must lie in the **open left half-plane** ($\text{Re}(p) < 0$).
- ✅ **PI removes offset** for step input; **P alone leaves a finite steady-state error**.
- ✅ **Derivative action** improves damping but **amplifies noise** — always use with a filter.
- ✅ **MATLAB vs Simulink:** Script-based control is best for linear analysis; Simulink is preferred for **nonlinear**, **time-varying**, or **event-driven** systems.
- ✅ **Common tunable parameters:** $K_c$ (controller gain), $\tau_I$ (reset time), $\tau_D$ (derivative time) — these correspond to the Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules covered in Module 4.
- ✅ **Performance vs Robustness trade-off:** Higher $K_c$ → faster response, less stability margin.

# Module 2 — Stability Analysis of Control Systems

## 1.1 Core Definition & Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability (KTU Definition):** A linear time-invariant (LTI) control system is said to be **stable** if and only if every bounded input produces a bounded output (BIBO stability). Equivalently, in the s-domain, *all poles of the closed-loop transfer function must lie strictly in the left-half of the s-plane* (i.e., have negative real parts). If any pole lies on the jω-axis, the system is **marginally stable**; if any pole lies in the right-half plane (RHP), the system is **unstable**.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Inverted Pendulum on a Cart"

Imagine balancing a broomstick on the palm of your hand.

- **Stable:** A ball resting at the bottom of a bowl — any small push causes oscillation that dies out, returning to equilibrium. Poles in the LHP.
- **Marginally (Critically) Stable:** A ball balanced perfectly on a flat table — it neither returns nor diverges; it stays where placed (or oscillates forever). Poles on the jω-axis.
- **Unstable:** A ball balanced on top of an inverted bowl — the slightest disturbance causes it to roll away and never return. Poles in the RHP.

The **controller** (your hand) must continually apply corrections to keep the broomstick (process) stable. **Stability analysis** answers the question: *"For what range of controller gain K will the broomstick not fall?"*

### 1.1.2 The Characteristic Equation — Gateway to All Stability Tests

Every stability method fundamentally examines the roots of the **closed-loop characteristic equation**:

$$1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)H(s) = 0$$

where:
- $G_c(s)$ = controller transfer function
- $G_p(s)$ = process transfer function
- $H(s)$ = feedback transfer function
- $K$ = loop gain (often a tunable parameter)

Expanding yields a polynomial in $s$:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Necessary Condition for Stability (KTU 2024 High-Yield):** *All coefficients of the characteristic equation must be present and have the same sign* (conventionally positive). If any coefficient is zero or negative, the system is **unstable or at best marginally stable**. This is necessary but **not sufficient** — sufficiency is provided by the Routh-Hurwitz test, root locus, or Bode criterion.

### 1.1.3 Classification of Stability Tests

| Method | Information Provided | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** | Stability / Instability; range of K for stability | Quick algebraic check, design of gain limits |
| **Root Locus Method** | Complete trajectory of closed-loop poles as K varies | Pole placement, transient response design |
| **Bode Stability Criterion** | Gain Margin (GM), Phase Margin (PM), relative stability | Frequency-domain robustness analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion determines the **number of roots** of the characteristic equation that lie in the **right-half of the s-plane** without actually solving for the roots.

### 2.1.1 Routh Array Construction Procedure

**Step 1:** Write the characteristic equation in standard form:
$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \dots + a_n = 0$$

**Step 2:** Construct the Routh array. The first two rows are filled directly from the coefficients (alternate columns). Subsequent rows are computed by determinants:

$$b_i = \frac{a_{i-1} \cdot a_{i+2} - a_i \cdot a_{i+1}}{a_{i-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Examine the **first column** of the Routh array:
- All positive → **Stable** (no RHP roots).
- Sign changes in first column = number of RHP roots → **Unstable**.

### 2.1.2 General Routh Array Layout

For a 4th-order polynomial $a_0 s^4 + a_1 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_3 s + a_4 = 0$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ |
| $s^3$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $c_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $d_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |

where:
$$b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 \cdot 0}{a_1} = a_4$$
$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}, \quad d_1 = c_1$$

### 2.1.3 Special Cases in Routh Array

> [!WARNING]
> **Case 1 — Zero in the First Column (only):** Replace the zero by a small positive number $\epsilon$ and continue, then take limit $\epsilon \to 0$.
>
> **Case 2 — Entire Row of Zeros:** Form the **auxiliary polynomial** from the row above, differentiate it with respect to $s$, and use its coefficients to replace the zero row. The roots of the auxiliary polynomial are symmetric about the origin.

### 2.1.4 Range of K (Stability Bound)

To find the range of gain $K$ for stability, set the $s^1$ row element to zero and solve for $K$. The boundary condition gives **critical K**; the **auxiliary equation** (from $s^2$ row) gives the **frequency of sustained oscillation**.

---

## 2.2 Root Locus Method

The root locus is the **locus (path) traced by the closed-loop poles** in the s-plane as the loop gain $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.

### 2.2.1 The Angle and Magnitude Conditions

The characteristic equation $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$ gives:

$$KG(s)H(s) = -1$$

This translates into two conditions:

**Angle Condition:**
$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180° (2q+1), \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

**Magnitude Condition:**
$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = \frac{1}{K}$$

### 2.2.2 Essential Rules for Sketching Root Locus (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Rule | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetry | Locus is symmetric about the real axis |
| 2 | Starting points (K = 0) | At the open-loop **poles** of $G(s)H(s)$ |
| 3 | Ending points (K → ∞) | At the open-loop **zeros** of $G(s)H(s)$ (or at infinity) |
| 4 | Number of branches | Equal to number of open-loop poles $P$ |
| 5 | Asymptote angles | $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180°}{P - Z}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ |
| 6 | Asymptote centroid ($\sigma_A$) | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{P - Z}$ |
| 7 | Real-axis segments | Odd number of poles + zeros to the right |
| 8 | Breakaway/break-in points | Roots of $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ on the locus |
| 9 | jω-axis crossing | Use Routh-Hurwitz to find $K_{max}$ and crossing frequency |
| 10 | Departure angle from complex pole | $\phi_d = 180° + \Sigma \phi_z - \Sigma \phi_p$ |

---

## 2.3 Bode Stability Criterion

The Bode stability criterion uses the **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** to quantify **relative stability** in the frequency domain.

### 2.3.1 Key Frequency-Domain Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Mathematical Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** | Frequency at which magnitude plot crosses 0 dB | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ |
| **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)** | Frequency at which phase plot crosses $-180°$ | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ |
| **Gain Margin (GM)** | Reciprocal of magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ |
| **Gain Margin in dB** | $GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} GM$ | $GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ |
| **Phase Margin (PM)** | Additional phase lag allowed at $\omega_{gc}$ | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ |

### 2.3.2 Bode Stability Criterion (KTU Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The closed-loop system is stable if and only if both:**
> 1. $GM_{dB} > 0$ (positive gain margin), AND
> 2. $PM > 0°$ (positive phase margin).
>
> For **good relative stability** in process control applications, the typical KTU-accepted benchmarks are:
> - $GM \geq 6 \text{ dB}$ (commonly $2$ to $6$ for chemical processes)
> - $PM = 30°$ to $60°$ (ideal: $45°$)

### 2.3.3 First-Order and Second-Order Magnitude Slopes (for Bode Sketching)

| Term in $G(s)$ | Magnitude Slope (dB/dec) | Phase Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| $K$ (constant) | $20 \log K$ (flat) | $0°$ |
| $1/s$ (pole at origin) | $-20$ | $-90°$ |
| $s$ (zero at origin) | $+20$ | $+90°$ |
| $\frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_1}$ (simple pole) | $0$ then $-20$ after $\omega_1$ | $0°$ → $-90°$ |
| $1 + s/\omega_1$ (simple zero) | $0$ then $+20$ after $\omega_1$ | $0°$ → $+90°$ |
| Quadratic $\frac{1}{(s/\omega_n)^2 + 2\zeta(s/\omega_n) + 1}$ | $-40$ after $\omega_n$ | $0°$ → $-180°$ |

---

> [!NOTE]
> This consolidated table is your one-stop reference for KTU 2024 Module 2 stability problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)H(s) = 0$ | Universal starting point |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Routh-Hurwitz Stability Test (with K)

> **Problem (KTU Typical):** Determine the range of $K$ for which the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function
> $$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$
> is stable. Also find the value of $K$ that causes sustained oscillation, and the frequency of oscillation.

### 3.1.1 Solution Walk-through

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop characteristic equation.**

With unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$:
$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \implies 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = 0$$

Multiplying through:
$$s(s+1)(s+2) + K = 0$$

Expanding:
$$s(s^2 + 3s + 2) + K = 0$$
$$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Routh array.**

The characteristic polynomial is $a_0 s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3 = 0$ with:
- $a_0 = 1$
- $a_1 = 3$
- $a_2 = 2$
- $a_3 = K$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $3$ | $K$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{3(2) - 1(K)}{3} = \dfrac{6 - K}{3}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 3 — Apply the stability condition (all first-column elements > 0).**

- From $s^1$ row: $\dfrac{6 - K}{3} > 0 \implies K < 6$
- From $s^0$ row: $K > 0$

**Range of stability:** $0 < K < 6$

**Step 4 — Find the critical K for sustained oscillation.**

At marginal stability, the $s^1$ row element becomes zero:
$$\dfrac{6 - K}{3} = 0 \implies K_{cr} = 6$$

**Step 5 — Find the frequency of oscillation using the auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row.**

The auxiliary equation is formed from the row just above the zero row:
$$3s^2 + K = 0$$

Substituting $K = 6$:
$$3s^2 + 6 = 0 \implies s^2 = -2 \implies s = \pm j\sqrt{2}$$

Therefore, the **frequency of sustained oscillation** is:
$$\omega_{osc} = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The system is stable for $0 < K < 6$. For $K = 6$, the system exhibits sustained oscillation at $\omega = \sqrt{2}$ rad/s. For $K > 6$, the system is unstable.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Root Locus Sketching (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** Sketch the root locus for $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$ and determine the range of $K$ for stability.

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Identify poles and zeros.**

- Open-loop poles: $s = 0, -2, -4$ (3 poles, $P = 3$)
- Open-loop zeros: none ($Z = 0$)
- Number of branches = 3
- Number of asymptotes = $P - Z = 3$

**Step 2 — Real-axis segments.**

Rule: A point on the real axis is on the locus if the total number of poles and zeros to its **right** is **odd**.

- Segment between $0$ and $-2$: poles to right = 1 (odd) ✓ on locus
- Segment between $-2$ and $-4$: poles to right = 2 (even) ✗ not on locus
- Segment left of $-4$: poles to right = 3 (odd) ✓ on locus

**Step 3 — Asymptote angles and centroid.**

Asymptote angles:
$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{P - Z} = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3}$$

- $q = 0$: $\phi_A = 60°$
- $q = 1$: $\phi_A = 180°$
- $q = 2$: $\phi_A = 300°$ (or $-60°$)

Centroid:
$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_i}{P - Z} = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

**Step 4 — Breakaway point (between $0$ and $-2$).**

From $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$:
$$K = -s(s+2)(s+4)$$

Differentiate with respect to $s$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -\frac{d}{ds}[s(s+2)(s+4)] = 0$$

Expand the cubic: $s(s+2)(s+4) = s(s^2 + 6s + 8) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s$

Derivative: $3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0$

Solve:
$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm 4\sqrt{3}}{6} = -2 \pm \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

Valid breakaway point (between $0$ and $-2$):
$$s_b = -2 + \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx -0.845$$

**Step 5 — jω-axis crossing using Routh-Hurwitz.**

Characteristic equation:
$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | $0$ |

For marginal stability: $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} = 0 \implies K = 48$

Auxiliary equation: $6s^2 + K = 0 \implies s^2 = -8 \implies s = \pm j2\sqrt{2}$

**Step 6 — Stability range.**

$0 < K < 48$ → Stable.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Verification:** Routh-Hurwitz ($K_{max} = 48$) and the jω-axis crossing of the root locus must agree — and they do. This is a frequent KTU follow-up question: *"Verify the stability range obtained from root locus using Routh-Hurwitz."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bode Stability Criterion

> **Problem:** For the open-loop transfer function
> $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$
> determine the stability of the closed-loop system using the Bode stability criterion. Find GM, PM, and comment on relative stability.

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Express in standard Bode (time-constant) form.**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s \cdot 5(s/5 + 1) \cdot 1(s+1)} = \frac{2}{s(s+1)(1 + s/5)}$$

Corner frequencies: $\omega_1 = 1$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s.
Low-frequency asymptote slope: $-20$ dB/dec (due to $1/s$).

**Step 2 — Magnitude plot — find Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$.**

Low-frequency magnitude:
$$M_{LF}(\omega) = 20 \log 2 - 20 \log \omega$$

For $\omega_{gc}$ (before $\omega = 1$, since slope is $-20$ dB/dec):
$$20 \log \frac{2}{\omega_{gc}} = 0 \implies \omega_{gc} = 2 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\omega_{gc} = 2 > \omega_1 = 1$, we must recheck. At $\omega = 1$, the slope changes to $-40$ dB/dec.

Recompute for $\omega > 1$:
$$M(\omega) = 20 \log \frac{2}{1} - 40 \log \frac{\omega}{1} = 6.02 - 40 \log \omega$$

Set $M(\omega_{gc}) = 0$:
$$40 \log \omega_{gc} = 6.02 \implies \log \omega_{gc} = 0.1505 \implies \omega_{gc} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/s}$$

This is $> 1$, so the answer is consistent. **$\omega_{gc} \approx 1.414$ rad/s.**

**Step 3 — Phase plot — find Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$.**

Phase angle:
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/5)$$

Set $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$:
$$-90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/5) = -180°$$
$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/5) = 90°$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}A + \tan^{-1}B = 90° \iff AB = 1$:
$$\omega_{pc} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{5} = 1 \implies \omega_{pc}^2 = 5 \implies \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$.**

At $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{2}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{1+\omega_{pc}^2}\sqrt{1+(\omega_{pc}/5)^2}}$$

Substituting $\omega_{pc}^2 = 5$:
$$= \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}\sqrt{1+5}\sqrt{1+1/5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}\sqrt{6}\sqrt{6/5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5} \cdot 6/\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Gain Margin.**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \frac{1}{1/3} = 3$$
$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log 3 = 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Phase Margin.**

At $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.414$ rad/s:
$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(1.414) - \tan^{-1}(1.414/5)$$
$$= -90° - 54.74° - 15.79° = -160.53°$$

$$PM = 180° + (-160.53°) = 19.47°$$

**Step 7 — Stability Verdict.**

- $GM_{dB} = 9.54 \text{ dB} > 0$ ✓
- $PM = 19.47° > 0°$ ✓

> **Conclusion:** The system is **stable**, but with **poor relative stability** (PM is below the ideal $45°$). The response will be oscillatory. To improve PM, we would either reduce the gain or add a lead compensator.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q:** Define the term *BIBO stability*. State the necessary condition for stability in terms of the coefficients of the characteristic equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **BIBO Stability:** A linear time-invariant system is said to be Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable if, for every bounded input, the output remains bounded. In the s-domain, this requires that **all poles of the closed-loop transfer function must lie in the left-half of the s-plane** (strictly negative real parts).
>
> **Necessary Condition:** All coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ of the characteristic equation
> $$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n = 0$$
> must be **present and have the same sign** (usually positive). If any coefficient is zero or negative, the system is unstable or at best marginally stable. (2 marks)
>
> However, this is only necessary; **sufficiency** is established by the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. (1 mark)

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q:** What is meant by *Gain Margin* and *Phase Margin*? What are the typical acceptable values for a well-designed control system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Gain Margin (GM):** It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be multiplied before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Mathematically, $GM = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ where $\omega_{pc}$ is the phase crossover frequency. Expressed in dB, $GM_{dB} = 20\log GM$. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before instability occurs. $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$. (1 mark)
>
> **Typical acceptable values:** $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM$ between $30°$ and $60°$ (ideal: $45°$). (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Construct the Routh array and determine the range of $K$ for stability of the system with characteristic equation:
$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 3K = 0$$
Find the value of $K$ that produces sustained oscillations, and the corresponding frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$
sketch the root locus and determine the range of $K$ for stability. Use the centroid and asymptote rules. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B-A (a)**

**Step 1 — Form the Routh array.**

Characteristic equation: $s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 3K = 0$

Coefficients: $a_0 = 1,\ a_1 = 2,\ a_2 = 8,\ a_3 = 4,\ a_4 = 3K$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $8$ | $3K$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $c_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $d_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |

Computing:
$$b_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot 4}{2} = \frac{16 - 4}{2} = 6$$
$$b_2 = \frac{2 \cdot 3K - 1 \cdot 0}{2} = 3K$$
$$c_1 = \frac{6 \cdot 4 - 2 \cdot 3K}{6} = \frac{24 - 6K}{6} = \frac{12 - 3K}{3} = 4 - K$$
$$d_1 = 3K$$

**Step 2 — Apply stability conditions.**

- $b_1 = 6 > 0$ ✓
- $c_1 = 4 - K > 0 \implies K < 4$
- $d_1 = 3K > 0 \implies K > 0$

**Range of stability:** $0 < K < 4$

**Step 3 — Critical K and frequency.**

At marginal stability, $c_1 = 0 \implies K = 4$.

Auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row: $b_1 s^2 + b_2 = 0 \implies 6s^2 + 3(4) = 0 \implies s^2 = -2$

$$\omega_{osc} = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

---

### **Solution to Question B-A (b)**

**Step 1 — Identify poles and zeros.**

- Poles: $s = 0, -1, -2$ → $P = 3$
- Zeros: $s = -3$ → $Z = 1$
- Number of asymptotes = $P - Z = 2$

**Step 2 — Asymptote angles and centroid.**

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{P - Z} = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{2}$$
- $q = 0$: $\phi_A = 90°$
- $q = 1$: $\phi_A = 270°$ (or $-90°$)

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 - 1 - 2) - (-3)}{3 - 1} = \frac{-3 + 3}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Real-axis segments (rule of odd count to the right).**

- Between $0$ and $-1$: 1 pole to right → on locus
- Between $-1$ and $-2$: 2 poles to right → not on locus
- Between $-2$ and $-3$: 3 (pole+pole+zero) to right → on locus
- Left of $-3$: 4 to right → not on locus

**Step 4 — jω-axis crossing using Routh-Hurwitz.**

Characteristic equation: $1 + \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = 0$

$$s(s+1)(s+2) + K(s+3) = 0$$
$$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + Ks + 3K = 0$$
$$s^3 + 3s^2 + (2+K)s + 3K = 0$$

Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $2+K$ |
| $s^2$ | $3$ | $3K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{3(2+K) - 3K}{3} = \dfrac{6 + 3K - 3K}{3} = 2$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $3K$ | $0$ |

Remarkably, the $s^1$ row is **independent of K** (= 2 > 0 always). The locus **never crosses the jω-axis** into the RHP.

**Range of stability:** $0 < K < \infty$ (stable for all positive $K$).

**Step 5 — Sketch description.**

The locus starts at the poles $0, -1, -2$. One branch ends at the zero $s = -3$ (along the real axis between $-2$ and $-3$). The remaining two branches depart into the complex plane and approach the asymptotes at $\pm 90°$ emanating from the origin. The locus does not enter the RHP.

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, investigate the stability of a system whose characteristic equation is
$$s^5 + s^4 + 2s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 5 = 0$$
Comment on the number of RHP poles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+10)}$$
determine, using the Bode stability criterion, the maximum value of $K$ for stable operation. Find the GM and PM at $K = 5$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B-B (a)**

**Step 1 — Construct the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^5$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $2$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $\dfrac{1\cdot2 - 1\cdot2}{1} = 0$ | $\dfrac{1\cdot3 - 1\cdot5}{1} = -2$ | $0$ |

**Special case — zero in first column.** Replace the $0$ with $\epsilon$ (small positive):

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $\epsilon$ | $-2$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{\epsilon(2) - 1(-2)}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{2\epsilon + 2}{\epsilon}$ | $\dfrac{\epsilon(5) - 1(0)}{\epsilon} = 5$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{\left(\frac{2\epsilon+2}{\epsilon}\right)(-2) - \epsilon(5)}{\frac{2\epsilon+2}{\epsilon}}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $5$ | $0$ | $0$ |

As $\epsilon \to 0^+$, the $s^2$ row first element $\to +\infty$, and the $s^1$ row first element evaluates to:

Numerator $\approx (\infty)(-2) \to -\infty$.

So $s^1$ first column $\to -$ value.

**First column inspection (taking limit $\epsilon \to 0$):**
- $s^5$: $1$ (+)
- $s^4$: $1$ (+)
- $s^3$: $\epsilon \to 0$ (+)
- $s^2$: $\to +\infty$ (+)
- $s^1$: $\to -$ (negative)
- $s^0$: $5$ (+)

**Sign changes:** $+ \to + \to + \to + \to - \to +$ → **Two sign changes**.

> **Conclusion:** The system has **2 poles in the right-half s-plane** and is therefore **unstable**. (1 mark)

---

### **Solution to Question B-B (b)**

**Step 1 — Put in time-constant form.**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s \cdot 10(s/10+1) \cdot 1(s+1)} = \frac{K/10}{s(s+1)(1+s/10)}$$

Let $K' = K/10$. For Bode analysis, the **gain** sets the magnitude but does not affect phase.

**Step 2 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$.**

Since the system is Type-1 (one pole at origin), the phase starts at $-90°$. With only one additional integrator from the simple poles, the phase reaches $-180°$ asymptotically.

**Phase equation:**
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/10)$$

Setting equal to $-180°$ would require $\omega \to \infty$. For finite $\omega$, the phase asymptotically approaches $-180°$ from above but never equals it. So $\omega_{pc} = \infty$ for this system — but practically, the system is stable for any finite $K$.

**Step 3 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ for $K = 5$.**

For $\omega < 1$ (slope $-20$ dB/dec, after the integrator at $s=0$):
$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log \frac{5/10}{\omega} = 20 \log(0.5) - 20 \log \omega$$
$$= -6.02 - 20 \log \omega$$

Setting to zero: $20 \log \omega_{gc} = -6.02 \implies \omega_{gc} \approx 0.5$ rad/s

Since $0.5 < 1$, this is consistent. **$\omega_{gc} \approx 0.5$ rad/s**.

**Step 4 — Phase Margin at $K = 5$.**

At $\omega_{gc} = 0.5$:
$$\angle G(j0.5) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5) - \tan^{-1}(0.05)$$
$$= -90° - 26.57° - 2.86° = -119.43°$$

$$PM = 180° - 119.43° = 60.57°$$

**Step 5 — Gain Margin.**

At $\omega_{pc} = \infty$, $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \to 0$, so $GM \to \infty$ dB.

> **Conclusion:** The system is **stable for any finite $K$**. At $K = 5$, $PM = 60.57°$ (excellent relative stability) and $GM = \infty$ dB. (1 mark for stability comment)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Stability Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the K terms in the Routh array** — When $K$ appears as a coefficient, students often drop it during array construction, leading to an incorrect "always stable" verdict. *Always carry K symbolically until the final inequality.*
> 2. **Skipping the auxiliary equation for oscillation frequency** — KTU specifically awards 1–2 marks for finding $\omega_{osc}$ at the critical $K$. Setting the $s^1$ element to zero gives $K_{cr}$, but the **auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row** is mandatory for $\omega_{osc}$.
> 3. **Asymptote angle formula misapplied** — Confusing $\pm 180°(2q+1)$ with $360°q$ is a frequent error. Remember: asymptotes for root locus (not root contours) are at odd multiples of $180°$ divided by $P - Z$.
> 4. **Bode plot sign errors in phase margin** — The formula is $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ (with the **negative** sign of the angle already included). Writing $180° - \vert \angle G \vert$ is the same thing but only when phase is between $-180°$ and $0°$.
> 5. **Confusing gain and phase crossover frequencies** — $\omega_{gc}$ is from the **magnitude** plot (where $|G| = 1$); $\omega_{pc}$ is from the **phase** plot (where $\angle G = -180°$). Reversing these swaps GM and PM.
> 6. **Forgetting units in GM** — GM is dimensionless (or in dB). Do not write "Newtons" or "rad/s" for GM.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Stability Analysis: Must-Know Summary**

- **Stability Definition:** BIBO → all closed-loop poles in LHP.
- **Necessary Condition:** All characteristic equation coefficients must be present and of the same sign.
- **Routh-Hurwitz:** Construct array from coefficients; count sign changes in first column to get number of RHP roots. Set $s^1$ row to zero to find critical $K$; use auxiliary equation for oscillation frequency.
- **Special Routh Cases:** Single zero → replace with $\epsilon$; entire row of zeros → auxiliary polynomial differentiated.
- **Root Locus:** Symmetric about real axis. Starts at poles ($K = 0$), ends at zeros or infinity. Asymptote angles = $(2q+1)180°/(P-Z)$. Centroid $\sigma_A = (\Sigma p - \Sigma z)/(P-Z)$. Real-axis rule: odd count of (poles + zeros) to the right.
- **Breakaway Points:** Roots of $dK/ds = 0$ on the locus.
- **jω-axis Crossing:** Use Routh-Hurwitz to find $K_{max}$ and crossing frequency.
- **Bode Stability Criterion:** System stable iff $GM_{dB} > 0$ AND $PM > 0°$.
- **Gain Margin:** $GM_{dB} = -20\log|G(j\omega_{pc})|$; **Phase Margin:** $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$.
- **Design Targets:** $GM \geq 6$ dB, $PM = 30°$ to $60°$ (ideal $45°$).
- **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check the stability range from Routh-Hurwitz with the jω-axis crossing of the root locus — they MUST agree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Select a suitable controller for the process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT604 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Select a suitable controller for the process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controller Selection:** Controller selection is the systematic engineering procedure of identifying the most appropriate feedback controller structure (P, PI, PD, or PID) and determining its optimal tuning parameters ($K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$) so that the closed-loop response satisfies the specified performance objectives — typically minimum integral error criteria (ISE, IAE, ITAE), acceptable overshoot, adequate stability margins, and satisfactory disturbance rejection — for a given process characterized by its gain ($K_p$), time constant ($\tau_p$), and dead time ($\theta_p$ or $t_d$).

In the KTU 2024 Process Dynamics and Control framework, **selection of a suitable controller** is treated as a three-step decision-making procedure:

1. **Identify** the process dynamics from open-loop or closed-loop tests.
2. **Match** the process behavior to a controller mode using empirical rules.
3. **Tune** the controller constants using established correlation methods (Ziegler–Nichols, Cohen–Coon, or Direct Synthesis).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **driving a car on a hilly road** and your goal is to maintain exactly **60 km/h**.

| Situation | Real-World Analogy | Controller Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Car responds *immediately* when you press the accelerator | Fast process, small dead time | **P (Proportional)** controller |
| Car has a *slight delay* before reacting; speed drifts slightly below target | Slow process with offset | **PI (Proportional + Integral)** controller |
| Car is *sluggish* and overshoots on hills | Process with large time constant and overshoot tendency | **PD (Proportional + Derivative)** controller |
| Car is sluggish **and** drifts away from target speed over time | Slow process with offset and overshoot | **PID (Full Three-Mode)** controller |

> [!NOTE]
> The controller is essentially the "steering wheel" of your chemical plant — selecting the wrong controller mode is like trying to drive a sports car with truck steering: technically functional but practically disastrous.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Process Gain $K_p$** — dimensionless or with engineering units.
- **Time Constant $\tau_p$** — in **minutes (min)**.
- **Dead Time $\theta_p$** — in **minutes (min)**.
- **Decay Ratio (DR)** — **¼ (quarter amplitude)** is the KTU standard.
- **Maximum Controller Gain $K_{u}$** — ultimate gain (closed-loop Z-N method).
- **Ultimate Period $P_u$** — period of sustained oscillation at $K_u$.
- **Performance Indices**: **ISE** (Integral of Squared Error), **IAE** (Integral of Absolute Error), **ITAE** (Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Controller Modes — Structural Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Controller

- **Output:** $p(t) = \bar{p} + K_c \, e(t)$
- **Transfer Function:** $G_c(s) = K_c$
- **Effect:** Increases speed of response, reduces (but does NOT eliminate) steady-state offset.
- **Offset:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{R}{1 + K_c K_p}$ (non-zero for finite $K_c$).

#### 2.1.2 Proportional + Integral (PI) Controller

- **Output:** $p(t) = \bar{p} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t)\,dt \right]$
- **Transfer Function:** $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} \right)$
- **Effect:** Eliminates steady-state offset; slows response slightly; may introduce oscillatory behavior.

#### 2.1.3 Proportional + Derivative (PD) Controller

- **Output:** $p(t) = \bar{p} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$
- **Transfer Function:** $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \tau_D s \right)$
- **Effect:** Anticipates future error; damps oscillations; **does NOT remove offset**.

#### 2.1.4 Proportional + Integral + Derivative (PID) Controller

- **Output (Ideal form):** $p(t) = \bar{p} + K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$
- **Transfer Function:** $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Used Form:** The **Ideal (Parallel) PID** form is used unless specified otherwise. The **Series (Cascade) PID** is given by $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)(1 + \tau_D s)$.

### 2.2 Selection Logic — How to Choose the Right Controller

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (Coughanowrt's Heuristic):** Choose the simplest controller that meets all performance specifications. Adding integral or derivative action introduces extra tuning parameters and potential instability.

#### 2.2.1 Empirical Selection Rules (Coughanowrt, 1991)

| Process Characteristic | Recommended Controller | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} < 0.1$ (small dead time) | **P** or **PI** | Fast dynamics, easy to control |
| $0.1 \le \dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} \le 1.0$ (moderate dead time) | **PI** or **PID** | Integral needed for offset removal; derivative for sluggish response |
| $\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} > 1.0$ (dominant dead time) | **PI only** (avoid D) | Derivative amplifies measurement noise on delayed signals |
| Integrating process (level control) | **P** or **PI** with anti-reset windup | Pure I action causes runaway |
| Noisy measurements | **PI** (avoid D) | Derivative action amplifies noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **integrating processes**, integral action must be removed or carefully limited because the process itself integrates — adding another integrator creates instability.

### 2.3 Performance Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

The optimal tuning minimizes a scalar performance index. The three most common are:

$$J_{ISE} = \int_0^{\infty} e^2(t)\, dt$$

$$J_{IAE} = \int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$$

$$J_{ITAE} = \int_0^{\infty} t \, \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$$

| Criterion | Error Weighting | Best Used For | Tuning Conservatism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISE** | Squares large errors | Quick damping, large initial errors | Least conservative |
| **IAE** | Linear in error | General-purpose tuning | Moderate |
| **ITAE** | Time-weighted (penalizes long-lasting errors) | Long-duration setpoint tracking | Most conservative, smallest overshoot |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_c(s) = K_c$ | P controller |
| 2 | $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | PI controller |
| 3 | $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \tau_D s\right)$ | PD controller |
| 4 | $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | Ideal PID |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)(\tau_D s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$ | Equivalent ideal PID numerator |
| 6 | $K_{c, \text{offset}} = \dfrac{R - e_{ss}}{K_p \, e_{ss}}$ | P-only offset compensation |
| 7 | $K_{u}$, $P_u$ from sustained oscillation | Closed-loop Z-N prerequisite |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p}$ | Process controllability index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Controller selection is applied in **every continuous process industry** — petroleum refineries (distillation column temperature control), pharmaceutical batch reactors (pH control), power plants (boiler drum level), and HVAC systems (room temperature). The wrong choice leads to oscillation, offset, equipment wear, and unsafe operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ziegler–Nichols Open-Loop Tuning Method

**Given:** A self-regulating FOPDT process
$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

**Step 1.** Apply a step change $\Delta U$ to the manipulated variable and record the open-loop response. Measure three parameters:
- $K_p$ = process steady-state gain = $\dfrac{\Delta Y_{ss}}{\Delta U}$
- $\tau_p$ = process time constant (time to reach 63.2% of final value)
- $\theta_p$ = apparent dead time

**Step 2.** Compute the dimensionless ratio $R = \dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p}$ (controllability ratio).

**Step 3.** Apply Ziegler–Nichols correlations (quarter-amplitude decay, DR = ¼):

$$\boxed{K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_I = \tau_p}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_D = \dfrac{\theta_p}{2}}$$

**For PI controller:**
$$K_c = \dfrac{0.9}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p}, \quad \tau_I = 3.33 \, \theta_p$$

**For P controller:**
$$K_c = \dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p \, \theta_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Z-N open-loop tuning is derived empirically for **quarter-amplitude decay** response. It tends to be **aggressive** — many practitioners multiply $K_c$ by 0.5 to obtain more conservative tuning.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Gain) Method

**Step 1.** Use a **P-only controller** in the closed loop. Set $\tau_I = \infty$ and $\tau_D = 0$.

**Step 2.** Increase $K_c$ gradually until the loop exhibits **sustained (marginal) oscillations** of constant amplitude.

**Step 3.** Record the critical values:
- $K_u$ = ultimate gain at which oscillations persist.
- $P_u$ = ultimate period of oscillation.

**Step 4.** Apply Z-N correlations:

| Controller | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ | $\tau_D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 1.2$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 2$ | $P_u / 8$ |

**Algebraic Justification for PI correlation:**
The closed-loop characteristic equation for PI control of a process with ultimate gain $K_u$ and ultimate period $P_u$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) &= 0 \\
1 + K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) G_p(s) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

At marginal stability, substitute $s = j\omega_u = j \dfrac{2\pi}{P_u}$ and equate real and imaginary parts to zero. Solving yields the empirical correlations above.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cohen–Coon Tuning Method

The Cohen–Coon method explicitly minimizes the **integrated error** assuming FOPDT dynamics and yields faster response than Z-N open-loop.

**For PI controller (most commonly used in industry):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p} \left[ 0.9 + \dfrac{\theta_p}{12 \, \tau_p} \right] \\
\tau_I &= \theta_p \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(\theta_p/\tau_p)}{9 + 20(\theta_p/\tau_p)}
\end{aligned}$$

**For PID controller:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta_p} \left[ \dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{\theta_p}{4 \, \tau_p} \right] \\
\tau_I &= \theta_p \cdot \dfrac{32 + 6(\theta_p/\tau_p)}{13 + 8(\theta_p/\tau_p)} \\
\tau_D &= \theta_p \cdot \dfrac{4}{11 + 2(\theta_p/\tau_p)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Direct Synthesis for FOPDT Process

**Goal:** Specify the desired closed-loop transfer function $\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{sp}}\right)_d$, then solve for $G_c$.

For a FOPDT process with desired first-order closed-loop response:

$$\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{sp}}\right)_d = \dfrac{e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_c \, s + 1}$$

**The controller is:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_c(s) &= \dfrac{1}{G_p(s)} \cdot \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{sp}}\right)_d}{1 - \left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{sp}}\right)_d} \\
&= \dfrac{(\tau_p s + 1)}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{e^{-\theta_p s}}{(\tau_c s + 1) - e^{-\theta_p s}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Approximation** using Pade' first-order for $e^{-\theta_p s} \approx \dfrac{1 - \theta_p s/2}{1 + \theta_p s/2}$:

$$\boxed{G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{(\tau_p s + 1)(\tau_c s + \frac{\theta_p}{2} + 1)}{\tau_c s \left(1 + \dfrac{\theta_p}{2} s\right) - 1}}$$

This rearranges into **PI or PID form** depending on assumptions about $\tau_c$ (desired closed-loop time constant), where the rule of thumb is **$\tau_c \ge \theta_p$** for realizable control.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Tuning Methods

| Method | Best For | Process Required | Tuning Conservatism | ISE Performance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Z-N Open-Loop | Quick field tuning | FOPDT (open-loop test) | Aggressive | High overshoot |
| Z-N Closed-Loop | Process with no dead time | Closed-loop experiment | Aggressive | ~25% overshoot |
| Cohen–Coon | FOPDT with $\theta_p / \tau_p > 0.3$ | FOPDT | Moderate | Better disturbance rejection |
| Direct Synthesis | Performance-specified design | Any model | User-controlled | Best setpoint tracking |
| ITAE Load Rejection | Disturbance rejection | FOPDT | Conservative | Minimal overshoot |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Feedback Control with Controller Selection Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spA[Setpoint R] --> sumA
    sumA{{Summing Junction}} --> eA[Error Signal e t]
    eA --> conA[Controller Gc s]
    conA -->|Manipulated Variable| mvA[Control Valve]
    mvA --> procA[Process Gp s]
    procA -->|Output Y| measA[Measurement Sensor]
    measA --> sumA
    procA --> dA[Disturbance D s]
    style sumA fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style conA fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style procA fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The summation junction sums the setpoint with the negative feedback measurement. The controller $G_c(s)$ receives $e(t)$ and produces $p(t)$ — the manipulated variable. This generic block diagram is **universal** across P, PI, PD, and PID; only the structure of $G_c(s)$ changes.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Controller Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Identify Process Dynamics] --> identA{Kp tauP thetaP known?}
    identA -->|Yes| typeA{Process Type?}
    identA -->|No| pulseA[Perform Pulse or Step Test]
    pulseA --> identA
    typeA -->|Self-Regulating FOPDT| ratioA{thetaP divide tauP ratio}
    typeA -->|Integrating| integA[Use P or PI with Anti-Reset Windup]
    ratioA -->|less than 0.1| pctrlA[Use P or PI Controller]
    ratioA -->|between 0.1 and 1.0| pictrlA[Use PI or PID Controller]
    ratioA -->|greater than 1.0| pictrl2A[Use PI Controller only]
    pctrlA --> tuneA{Choose Tuning Method}
    pictrlA --> tuneA
    pictrl2A --> tuneA
    tuneA --> znA[Ziegler Nichols for Quarter Decay]
    tuneA --> ccA[Cohen Coon for Faster Response]
    tuneA --> dsA[Direct Synthesis for Specified Performance]
    znA --> validA[Validate in Closed-Loop Simulation]
    ccA --> validA
    dsA --> validA
    validA --> fineA[Fine-Tune Kc tauI tauD on Plant]
    fineA --> doneA[Controller Commissioned]
    style startA fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style doneA fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style validA fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Four Controller Mode Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    errB[Error e s] --> modeB{Selected Controller Mode}
    modeB -->|P| kcB[Multiply by Kc]
    modeB -->|PI| piB[Parallel PI Block]
    modeB -->|PD| pdB[Parallel PD Block]
    modeB -->|PID| pidB[Parallel PID Block]
    piB --> piOut[Output Kc times e plus integral]
    pdB --> pdOut[Output Kc times e plus derivative]
    pidB --> pidOut[Output Kc times e plus integral plus derivative]
    kcB --> pOut[Output Kc times e only]
    style errB fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style modeB fill:#fbf,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Mapping — Selection Rule Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ruleA[thetaP over tauP less than 0.1] --> outA[Choice P or PI]
    ruleB[0.1 to 1.0] --> outB[Choice PI or PID]
    ruleC[greater than 1.0] --> outC[Choice PI only]
    ruleD[Noisy Process] --> outD[Choice PI only avoid D]
    ruleE[Integrating Process] --> outE[Choice P or PI with Anti Windup]
    style ruleA fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style ruleB fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style ruleC fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style ruleD fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style ruleE fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> *List the four standard feedback controllers and state one specific advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Controller | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Simple, fast response | Leaves a permanent steady-state offset |
| **PI** | Eliminates offset completely | Can introduce oscillations and overshoot |
| **PD** | Anticipates future error, reduces overshoot | Amplifies measurement noise, no offset removal |
| **PID** | Best overall performance — offset removal + fast response | Three tuning parameters, more complex tuning |

**Allocation:** 0.5 marks per advantage + 0.5 marks per disadvantage × 4 = 3 marks. KTU key requires a clear table — bullet lists lose marks.

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *What is the controllability ratio $\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p}$? How does its value influence controller selection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **controllability ratio** $R = \dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p}$ is the ratio of the **dead time** to the **time constant** of a FOPDT process. It quantifies how difficult a process is to control.

- If $R < 0.1$: process has **fast dynamics** relative to dead time → easy to control → **P or PI** suffices.
- If $0.1 \le R \le 1.0$: **moderate difficulty** → use **PI or PID**.
- If $R > 1.0$: **dominant dead time** → difficult to control → use **PI only**, avoid derivative action because it amplifies the noise of delayed signals.

**Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + three ranges with controller choice (1.5 marks) + practical example (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Ziegler–Nichols open-loop tuning method** for a FOPDT process. Derive the correlations for P, PI, and PID controllers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control loop on a heat exchanger yields the following open-loop step test data:
- Step in steam flow: $\Delta U = 5\%$ opening
- Steady-state temperature rise: $\Delta Y_{ss} = 12^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Time constant: $\tau_p = 6 \text{ min}$
- Apparent dead time: $\theta_p = 1.5 \text{ min}$

Select a suitable controller and determine its tuning constants using the Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method. Comment on whether derivative action should be used. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method assumes the process is described by a First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) model:

$$\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta_p s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

A step change $\Delta U$ is applied; the response yields $K_p$, $\tau_p$, $\theta_p$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Empirical correlations for quarter-amplitude decay response:

For **P controller:**
$$K_c = \dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p \, \theta_p}$$

For **PI controller:**
$$K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \, \tau_p}{K_p \, \theta_p}, \quad \tau_I = 3.33 \, \theta_p$$

For **PID controller:**
$$K_c = \dfrac{1.2 \, \tau_p}{K_p \, \theta_p}, \quad \tau_I = 2 \, \theta_p, \quad \tau_D = \dfrac{\theta_p}{2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** These correlations were derived empirically by Ziegler and Nichols (1942) to give a closed-loop response with a **decay ratio of ¼** (each successive overshoot is ¼ of the previous one). The tuning tends to be **aggressive** with significant overshoot, so industrial practice often multiplies $K_c$ by 0.5 for safer operation.

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute Process Parameters:**

$$K_p = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{ss}}{\Delta U} = \dfrac{12^{\circ}\text{C}}{5\%} = 2.4 \dfrac{{}^{\circ}\text{C}}{\%}$$

$$\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} = \dfrac{1.5}{6.0} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Controller Selection:**

Since $0.1 < \dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} = 0.25 < 1.0$ and $\dfrac{\theta_p}{\tau_p} < 0.3$ (Cohen–Coon threshold), the process has moderate dead time. **A PI controller is recommended** (or PID if faster response is needed, but temperature loops often have noisy thermocouple signals that discourage derivative action).

**Step 3 (4 marks) — Apply Z-N PI Correlations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \dfrac{0.9 \, \tau_p}{K_p \, \theta_p} = \dfrac{0.9 \times 6.0}{2.4 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{5.4}{3.6} = 1.5 \dfrac{\%}{{}^{\circ}\text{C}} \\
\tau_I &= 3.33 \, \theta_p = 3.33 \times 1.5 = 5.0 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Comment on Derivative Action:**

Derivative action is **not recommended** here because: (i) temperature measurements from thermocouples are noisy, and derivative amplifies noise; (ii) the process dead time is comparable to typical measurement delays. Hence **PI control with $K_c = 1.5 \%/{}^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $\tau_I = 5.0$ min** is the appropriate selection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in $K_c$ (must show $\%/{}^{\circ}\text{C}$ or equivalent).
> 2. **Not justifying controller choice** — merely calculating $K_c$ without explaining *why* PI over P or PID loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Z-N formula** — confusing PI and PID correlations (especially the factor 1.2 vs 0.9 in $K_c$).
> 4. **Not commenting on derivative action** when the question asks — many students stop at the calculation.

---

#### **Question B-B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define **ISE, IAE, and ITAE** performance indices. Compare them and state which one gives the most conservative tuning for a setpoint-tracking application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A level control loop on a tank shows the following closed-loop behavior: with a proportional-only controller, sustained oscillations of period **$P_u = 4$ min** appear when the gain is raised to **$K_u = 8$**. Select and tune a suitable controller using the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop method. Justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definitions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{ISE} &= \int_0^{\infty} e^2(t)\, dt \\
J_{IAE} &= \int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt \\
J_{ITAE} &= \int_0^{\infty} t \, \vert e(t) \vert \, dt
\end{aligned}$$

where $e(t)$ is the error between setpoint and measured process variable.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Comparison:**

| Index | Penalizes | Tuning Result | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISE** | Large initial errors heavily | Less conservative, faster response | When large early errors are unacceptable |
| **IAE** | All errors equally | Moderate conservatism | General-purpose tuning |
| **ITAE** | Long-duration errors heavily | Most conservative, smallest overshoot | Long-duration setpoint tracking |

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Conclusion:**

For **setpoint tracking**, **ITAE** gives the most conservative tuning because the time-weighting $t$ heavily penalizes any error that persists for a long time, forcing the controller to settle quickly. ISE ignores duration, so it tends to allow longer settling times. ITAE-based tuning typically results in the smallest overshoot and the smoothest response, although the initial response may be slightly slower.

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Identify Process Type:**

Sustained oscillations with P-only control indicate the process is **self-regulating and stable** (otherwise sustained oscillations would not occur). The dead time is moderate since the closed-loop experiment converged to a stable oscillation period.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Controller Selection:**

For a **level control loop** with moderate dynamics, a **PI controller** is the standard choice. (P-only would leave a permanent offset in level; PID is unnecessary because level loops are typically slow and not noise-sensitive.)

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Apply Z-N Closed-Loop PI Correlations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 8 = 3.6 \\
\tau_I &= \dfrac{P_u}{1.2} = \dfrac{4}{1.2} = 3.33 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Tuning:** **PI controller with $K_c = 3.6$ and $\tau_I = 3.33$ min**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing open-loop and closed-loop Z-N** — these are two **different** methods with different formulas. Stating $K_c = 1.2 \tau_p/(K_p \theta_p)$ when the question clearly says *closed-loop method* is a common error.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 0.45 vs 0.6** — PI uses 0.45, PID uses 0.6 of $K_u$.
> 3. **Not justifying selection** — choosing P-only without explanation loses marks.
> 4. **Wrong $\tau_I$ formula** — PI uses $P_u / 1.2$, PID uses $P_u / 2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Controller Selection**

- **Always start by identifying the process:** determine $K_p$, $\tau_p$, and $\theta_p$ from a step test (open-loop) OR $K_u$ and $P_u$ from a closed-loop test.
- **Use the controllability ratio $R = \theta_p / \tau_p$** as your primary decision metric:
  - $R < 0.1$ → **P or PI**
  - $0.1 \le R \le 1.0$ → **PI or PID**
  - $R > 1.0$ → **PI only**
- **Avoid derivative action** when (i) measurements are noisy, (ii) dead time is dominant, (iii) the process is already second-order or higher.
- **For integrating processes** (level control without outflow pump), use **P-only** or **PI with anti-reset windup** — never use pure integral control.
- **Z-N open-loop method:** $K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \tau_p}{K_p \theta_p}$, $\tau_I = 3.33 \theta_p$ (PI).
- **Z-N closed-loop method:** $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $\tau_I = P_u / 1.2$ (PI).
- **Cohen–Coon method:** Faster response than Z-N; best for FOPDT processes with $\theta_p / \tau_p > 0.3$.
- **Direct Synthesis method:** Best when performance specifications (closed-loop time constant $\tau_c$) are explicitly given.
- **Always validate tuning in simulation** before plant implementation.
- **Quarter-amplitude decay (DR = ¼)** is the KTU standard target for Z-N tuning.
- **Industrial rule of thumb:** After Z-N tuning, multiply $K_c$ by 0.5 to obtain a more conservative, safer operation.
- **Performance Indices:** ISE < IAE < ITAE in terms of conservatism (ITAE most conservative, smallest overshoot).
- **Units of $K_c$ must match** the units of the manipulated variable and the controlled variable (e.g., $\%/{}^{\circ}\text{C}$, kg/s·m, etc.).
- **Cascade control:** If inner loop is fast (flow) and outer loop is slow (temperature), use PI for inner, P or PI for outer.
- **Anti-reset windup** is mandatory whenever integral action is used on a process that can saturate (e.g., valve at 0% or 100%).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimize the control parameter for desired performance using Ziegler-Nichols tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT604 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimize the control parameter for desired performance using Ziegler-Nichols tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ziegler–Nichols (Z–N) tuning** is an empirical, heuristic controller-tuning methodology developed by John G. Ziegler and Nathaniel B. Nichols (1942) that determines optimal Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) controller parameters — namely the **proportional gain $K_c$**, **integral time $\tau_I$ (or $T_i$)**, and **derivative time $\tau_D$ (or $T_d$)** — to achieve a specific closed-loop time-domain performance, classically a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio (QADR)** per successive peak of the error response.

The technique provides two distinct procedures:
- **Method-1 (Open-Loop Reaction Curve):** A step change is applied to the manipulated variable; the resulting S-shaped process reaction curve yields the **process gain $K$**, **dead time $L$**, and **process time constant $T$**.
- **Method-2 (Closed-Loop Ultimate Gain):** With a pure proportional controller, the gain is increased until the loop produces **sustained sinusoidal oscillations** of period $P_u$, identifying the **ultimate gain $K_u$** and **ultimate period $P_u$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Definition (Memorise):**
> *“Ziegler–Nichols tuning is an experimental correlation technique that prescribes PID settings based on the open-loop process reaction curve or the closed-loop ultimate gain such that the closed-loop response exhibits a quarter-decay ratio, providing a fast but slightly oscillatory response suitable for set-point tracking in industrial process control.”*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — “The Hot Shower Problem”

Imagine stepping into a shower with two knobs — *flow* (proportional) and *memory* (integral). If you twist the **flow knob** too hard, the water oscillates between ice-cold and scalding (over-damped → unstable). If you twist too softly, you stay lukewarm forever (over-damped → sluggish). 

**Ziegler–Nichols tuning** is the “engineering recipe” that tells you exactly how much to turn each knob to get the water temperature stable in **4 oscillations of decreasing quarter-size** — fast enough to feel responsive, slow enough to feel safe.

| Engineering Element | Shower Analogy |
|---|---|
| Process (shower + plumbing) | Plumbing & pipe length |
| Controller (you) | The person holding the knob |
| Manipulated Variable (MV) | Hot/cold water flow |
| Controlled Variable (CV) | Water temperature at showerhead |
| Ultimate Gain $K_u$ | The knob angle that causes water to swing wildly |
| $K_c$ from Z–N | The safe knob angle ≈ $0.6 \times K_u$ |
| Integral action | Remembers past temperature errors |
| Derivative action | Anticipates future swings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Z–N tuning deliberately gives an **aggressive, oscillatory** closed-loop response. It is *not* optimal for minimum IAE/ITAE — it is optimal for **fast disturbance rejection** in lag-dominant processes. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly recognises this trade-off.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Reference Metrics & Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $K_c$ | Controller proportional gain | dimensionless |
| $\tau_I \; (\text{or } T_i)$ | Integral (reset) time | **minutes** |
| $\tau_D \; (\text{or } T_d)$ | Derivative time | **minutes** |
| $K$ | Open-loop process gain | $\dfrac{\%\, \text{PV}}{\%\, \text{MV}}$ or $\dfrac{\text{units}}{\text{units}}$ |
| $L$ | Apparent dead time | **minutes** |
| $T$ | Process first-order time constant | **minutes** |
| $K_u$ | Ultimate gain (closed-loop) | dimensionless |
| $P_u$ | Ultimate period of oscillation | **minutes** |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio (Z–N → ~0.21) | dimensionless |
| Quarter Decay Ratio | Successive peak ratio = **1/4** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Control Philosophy

Z–N tuning is grounded on **two engineering insights**:

1. **Most industrial processes can be approximated by a First-Order-Plus-Dead-Time (FOPDT) model:**
   $$G_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$$
   This captures > **85%** of single-loop regulatory control loops in chemical plants.

2. **A good controller should reject load disturbances rapidly, accepting modest overshoot.** The chosen benchmark is a **quarter decay ratio** — i.e., each successive overshoot peak is **25%** of the previous one. This corresponds to a damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.21$ (under-damped but stable).

---

## 2.2 Method-1: Open-Loop Reaction Curve (Process Reaction Curve)

### 2.2.1 Experimental Procedure
1. Place the loop in **manual** mode with the process at steady state.
2. Apply a **step change $\Delta M$** to the controller output (MV).
3. Record the process variable $c(t)$ until it reaches a new steady state $\Delta C$.
4. On the recorded curve, draw:
   - A horizontal line at the **final steady-state value**.
   - A **tangent line** through the **inflection point** (point of maximum slope) of the S-curve.
5. The intersection of the tangent with the initial and final value lines yields:
   - **Dead time $L$** (horizontal distance from step input to tangent’s first intersection).
   - **Time constant $T$** (horizontal distance between the two tangent intersections).
6. Process gain: $\, K = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta M}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a tangent at the inflection point?** The S-curve inflection point represents the region where the dead-time lag transitions into the dominant first-order lag — it is the most linear portion of the dynamic response, allowing a clean two-parameter identification.

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Method-1

| Controller Type | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ | $\tau_D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $\dfrac{T}{K L}$ | $\infty$ | $0$ |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{0.9 \, T}{K L}$ | $\dfrac{L}{0.3}$ | $0$ |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1.2 \, T}{K L}$ | $2 L$ | $0.5 \, L$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE:** The KTU board often tests the **classical PID row** specifically. Memorise:
> $$K_c^{PID} = 1.2 \frac{T}{KL}, \quad \tau_I = 2L, \quad \tau_D = 0.5L$$

---

## 2.3 Method-2: Closed-Loop Ultimate Gain (Continuous Cycling Method)

### 2.3.1 Experimental Procedure
1. Place the controller in **automatic** mode with $\tau_I = \infty$ and $\tau_D = 0$ (pure P-control).
2. Set $K_c$ to a small value; introduce a small set-point disturbance.
3. Gradually **increase $K_c$** until the process variable exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude sinusoidal oscillations**.
4. Record the controller gain at this point as **$K_u$ (ultimate gain)** and the oscillation period as **$P_u$ (ultimate period)**.
5. Reduce $K_c$ to a safe operating value immediately to avoid equipment damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety:** Sustained cycling can damage final control elements (valves, pumps). Always perform on simulation or with operator-supervised steps on plant.

### 2.3.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Method-2

| Controller Type | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ | $\tau_D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | $\infty$ | $0$ |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{1.2}$ | $0$ |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, P_u$ | $0.125 \, P_u$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students confuse $\tau_I$ and $\tau_D$ values. Recall the **PID mnemonic “6-3-1.5”** — divide $P_u$ by **2** for $\tau_I$ and by **8** for $\tau_D$ (i.e., $0.5 P_u$ and $0.125 P_u$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Z–N Tuning |
|---|---|
| Petroleum Refining | Distillation column temperature control |
| HVAC Systems | Building zone temperature regulation |
| Chemical Reactors | Jacket coolant flow control |
| Power Plants | Boiler drum level and steam pressure |
| Pulp & Paper | Stock flow and consistency loops |
| Water Treatment | pH neutralisation loops |

**Limitation:** Z–N is poor for **integrating processes** (pure tanks, level control) and **inverse-response processes**. KTU 2024 syllabus recommends Cohen–Coon or Direct Synthesis for such cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Method-1 (Open-Loop) Formulas — Conceptual Basis

The PID controller output in *parallel (ideal) form* is:
$$m(t) = K_c\!\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\sigma)\,d\sigma + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Transfer function of the ideal PID controller:
$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right] = K_c \frac{\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$$

Open-loop process (FOPDT approximation):
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$$

Closed-loop transfer function (for set-point tracking):
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

Ziegler and Nichols correlated empirical tuning constants by simulating the closed-loop response of a wide class of FOPDT processes and choosing $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$ such that the **quarter-decay ratio** criterion was satisfied. The correlations that emerged are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P:} \quad K_c &= \frac{T}{KL}, \quad \tau_I = \infty, \quad \tau_D = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{PI:} \quad K_c &= 0.9 \frac{T}{KL}, \quad \tau_I = \frac{L}{0.3}, \quad \tau_D = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{PID:} \quad K_c &= 1.2 \frac{T}{KL}, \quad \tau_I = 2L, \quad \tau_D = 0.5L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Heuristic interpretation:**
- For pure P, the loop gain $K_c K$ must compensate the dead time $L$ relative to time constant $T$ — hence $K_c = T/(KL)$.
- For PID, the derivative term provides phase lead that anticipates the dead-time delay, allowing the gain to be boosted by **20%** ($1.2\times$) and the integral to act **twice** as fast ($\tau_I = 2L$).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Open-Loop Reaction Curve (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem Statement:**
A step change of $\Delta M = 10\%$ is applied to the MV of a temperature control loop. The PV responds with a steady-state change of $\Delta C = 25^\circ\text{C}$. The tangent at the inflection point intersects the initial value line at $t = 1.5\,\text{min}$ and the final value line at $t = 4.5\,\text{min}$. Apply the Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method to determine the settings of a **PID controller**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute process gain $K$:**
$$K = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta M} = \frac{25^\circ \text{C}}{10\%} = 2.5 \;\; \frac{^\circ\text{C}}{\%}$$

**Step 2 — Compute dead time $L$ (horizontal distance from step to first intersection):**
$$L = 1.5 \;\; \text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Compute time constant $T$ (horizontal distance between the two intersections):**
$$T = 4.5 - 1.5 = 3.0 \;\; \text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Z–N PID correlations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 1.2 \cdot \frac{T}{K L} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{3.0}{2.5 \times 1.5} \\[4pt]
    &= 1.2 \cdot \frac{3.0}{3.75} = 1.2 \times 0.8 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.96 \;\; \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\tau_I = 2L = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \;\; \text{min}
$$

$$
\tau_D = 0.5L = 0.5 \times 1.5 = 0.75 \;\; \text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{K_c = 0.96, \quad \tau_I = 3.0 \;\text{min}, \quad \tau_D = 0.75 \;\text{min}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Closed-Loop Ultimate Gain (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A pure-proportional controller is placed on a level-control loop. As $K_c$ is increased, sustained oscillations first appear at $K_c = 8.4$ with a period of $2.6$ minutes. Recommend **PI** and **PID** settings using Z–N closed-loop tuning.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify $K_u$ and $P_u$:**
$$K_u = 8.4, \qquad P_u = 2.6 \;\; \text{min}$$

**Step 2 — PI Controller Settings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 8.4 = 3.78 \\[4pt]
\tau_I &= \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{2.6}{1.2} = 2.167 \;\; \text{min} \\[4pt]
\tau_D &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — PID Controller Settings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 8.4 = 5.04 \\[4pt]
\tau_I &= 0.5 \, P_u = 0.5 \times 2.6 = 1.3 \;\; \text{min} \\[4pt]
\tau_D &= 0.125 \, P_u = 0.125 \times 2.6 = 0.325 \;\; \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\text{PI: } K_c = 3.78,\; \tau_I = 2.167\;\text{min} \quad\quad \text{PID: } K_c = 5.04,\; \tau_I = 1.3\;\text{min},\; \tau_D = 0.325\;\text{min}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Method-1 (Open-Loop) | Method-2 (Closed-Loop) |
|---|---|---|
| Experiment Type | Open-loop step test | Closed-loop trial |
| Parameters Identified | $K, L, T$ | $K_u, P_u$ |
| Requires Sustained Cycling? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Safe for Plant Use? | **Highly safe** | **Risky — operator supervised** |
| Works for Slow Processes? | Slow (waits for steady state) | Fast |
| Information Obtained | Gain + Dead time + Time constant | Only two scalars |
| Best Suited For | Self-regulating processes | Any SISO loop |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **High** |
| Tuning Basis | Quarter-decay ratio | Quarter-decay ratio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Z–N Tuning — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint R] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[PID Controller Gc]
    C --> D[Control Valve MV]
    D --> E[Process Plant Gp]
    E --> F[Sensor Measurement]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Output PV C]

    subgraph tun["Tuning Block"]
        H[Identify Ku] --> I[Identify Pu]
```
